title.en: JSON vs XML: Which Data Format Is Right for Your Project?

description.en: JSON vs XML comparison — verbosity, parsing, schema validation, namespaces, use cases in APIs and enterprise systems, and when to use each format.

intro.en: JSON and XML are both text-based data formats used for storing and transmitting structured data. JSON has become the dominant format for web APIs since the early 2010s, largely replacing XML. However, XML retains significant advantages in document-oriented and enterprise contexts where schema validation, namespaces, XSLT transformation, and mixed content are required.

a.en: Natively maps to programming language objects and arrays

b.en: Mixed content (text + elements in same node)

a.en: JSON Schema is less mature than XSD

b.en: Very verbose — tags repeated for every element
Harder to write and read by hand
No native browser data type equivalent

a.en: Use JSON for REST APIs, web application data, configuration, mobile app backends, microservices communication, and any modern data interchange scenario.

b.en: Use XML for SOAP web services (legacy), document formats (DOCX, SVG, RSS, Atom), enterprise integrations (EDI, banking, healthcare), configuration with schema validation requirements (Maven, Ant), and any context where mixed content, namespaces, or XSLT are needed.

performanceNote.en: JSON payloads are typically 30–50% smaller than equivalent XML payloads due to the absence of repeated tag names. JSON also parses faster because its grammar is simpler. For high-volume APIs, this difference in bandwidth and CPU cost is significant.

a.en: Native via JSON.parse() in all browsers. No external library needed.

b.en: DOMParser and XMLSerializer built into all browsers. XPath also supported natively.

verdict.en: For new web APIs: JSON. For enterprise integrations, document processing, or systems requiring mature schema validation and transformation: XML. If you are working with legacy SOAP services or document formats (Office files, SVG), you will encounter XML — a YAML or JSON equivalent does not exist for these domains.

question.en: Why did JSON replace XML for APIs?

answer.en: JSON won because it is simpler, more concise, faster to parse, and maps directly to JavaScript objects — which is critical for browser-based applications. When REST APIs emerged in the 2000s, JSON was the obvious choice over the verbose and complex XML required by SOAP.

No. XML is the format underpinning Microsoft Office documents (DOCX, XLSX), SVG images, RSS and Atom feeds, Android resource files, Maven and Gradle build files, many enterprise integration protocols, and healthcare data standards (HL7 FHIR). It is less used for new API development but remains essential in many domains.
